title: The Faithful Parent 7: Parenting from Conception (Cont'd)
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 01-28-24 - The Faithful Parent 7 - Parenting from Conception Cont'd - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Review and Continuation: Repentance and the Sin of Indifference
subpoints: Review: the baby in the womb is a full person made in the image of God, from conception
The church must repent from the sin of indifference to the sanctity of life
Indifference is not neutrality — to do nothing while the innocent are being killed is sin

outline.title: 1. The Call to Rescue: Proverbs 24:11–12
subpoints: 'Rescue those being led away to death; hold back those staggering toward slaughter'
'If you say, I did not know about this — does not He who weighs the heart perceive it?'
God holds us accountable for the knowledge we possess and suppress
The excuse of ignorance does not stand before God who weighs the heart

outline.title: 2. Practical Ways to Rescue
subpoints: Pray — for mothers in crisis, for the church, for legislators, for a change in culture
Vote — use the civic tools God has given us to protect innocent life
Volunteer at pregnancy resource centers — give time and money
Counsel — train to come alongside women in crisis pregnancies
Adopt and foster — open your home to children whose mothers chose life
Give financially to pro-life organizations doing frontline work
Knock on legislators' doors — the church's voice must be heard in the public square

outline.title: 3. The Ultimate Solution Is the Gospel
subpoints: The pro-life movement needs Christ — political victory alone does not change hearts
A culture that devalues life needs the gospel that gives life ultimate value
The church's pro-life work must be accompanied by gospel proclamation

outline.title: 4. Five Foundational Expectations for Faithful Parenting
subpoints: The Bible is sufficient — God's Word is all we need to raise children faithfully
Discipline does not produce regeneration — only the Spirit gives new birth; parents plant, God gives growth
Child-rearing is covenantal — we raise our children as members of God's covenant community
Fathers bear final responsibility — not mothers alone, not both equally; the father is accountable
Young children are not equipped for independence — do not treat small children as autonomous decision-makers

summary: Pastor Tito Lyro continues the previous lesson on the sanctity of life, applying Proverbs 24:11-12 as a call to actively rescue those being led to death. He lists seven practical responses (pray, vote, volunteer, counsel, adopt, give, advocate) and emphasizes that only the gospel ultimately transforms a culture of death. He then introduces five foundational expectations that will govern the rest of the Faithful Parent series: the sufficiency of Scripture, the limits of discipline, the covenantal framework of child-rearing, the primacy of the father's responsibility, and the dependence of young children.
transcript:
## Review: Last Week's Lesson

Last week we established that parenting begins at conception. The baby in the womb is a full human being — not merely a potential person, but a person — from the moment of conception. We grounded this in the image of God (Genesis 9), the Mosaic law's protection of unborn life (Exodus 21), and the testimony of the Psalms and Prophets.

Today I want to do two things: complete the application from last week, and introduce five foundational expectations that will frame the rest of this series.

## The Sin of Indifference

Before we talk about what to *do*, we must name what we have *failed to do*. The church in America has largely been silent on the abortion issue. We have treated it as political rather than biblical, as someone else's concern rather than our own. This silence is not neutrality — it is sin.

The child in the womb is your neighbor. And you are called to love your neighbor. Silence in the face of slaughter is not love.

## Proverbs 24:11–12: The Call to Rescue

> "Rescue those being led away to death; hold back those staggering toward slaughter. If you say, 'But we knew nothing about this,' does not He who weighs the heart perceive it? Does not He who guards your life know it? Will He not repay everyone according to what they have done?" (Proverbs 24:11–12)

God commands rescue. He does not permit passivity. And He does not accept the excuse of ignorance — He who weighs the heart knows exactly what we knew and when we knew it.

### Seven Practical Ways to Rescue

**Pray.** Before all else, pray. Pray for mothers in crisis. Pray for the church to wake up. Pray for legislators. Pray for a change in the culture of death.

**Vote.** God has given us a democratic form of government. This is a civic tool, and we are accountable for how we use it. Elect officials who will protect innocent life.

**Volunteer.** Pregnancy resource centers are doing the work in our communities. Give your time. Show up. Man a phone line. Drive a woman to an appointment.

**Counsel.** Many centers train volunteers in pregnancy counseling. You can be the person who walks alongside a woman in crisis and helps her choose life.

**Adopt and foster.** For many mothers, the fear that the child will have no home is a factor in their decision. Open your home.

**Give financially.** Pro-life organizations, pregnancy centers, and maternity homes cannot operate without funding. Give generously.

**Knock on legislators' doors.** The church's voice must be heard in the public square. Call your representatives. Write letters. Show up at hearings.

## The Ultimate Solution Is the Gospel

All of these are good and necessary. But they are not sufficient on their own. A culture that devalues life will not be transformed by political victory alone. Hearts must change — and only the gospel changes hearts.

Our pro-life work and our gospel proclamation are not in tension. They belong together. We rescue because we believe every person is made in God's image. And we tell the good news because only Christ can ultimately heal what is broken.

## Five Foundational Expectations for Faithful Parenting

With the next lesson we will move to the topic of parenting teenagers. But first, I want to lay out five foundational expectations that will shape everything else I say in this series. These are convictions, not techniques.

### 1. The Bible Is Sufficient

Everything you need to raise your children faithfully is in the Word of God. You do not need the latest parenting book — though there are helpful ones. You do not need a degree in child psychology. You need to know and apply what God has revealed.

### 2. Discipline Does Not Produce Regeneration

Parents sometimes believe that if they do everything right, their children will automatically be saved. This is not what the Bible teaches. Regeneration is the work of the Holy Spirit, not the fruit of faithful parenting. You plant and water; God gives the growth. This is liberating — it means your child's salvation does not rest on your perfection. It also keeps you humble.

### 3. Child-Rearing Is Covenantal

We raise our children as members of God's covenant community. They are not strangers to the covenant — they are raised within it, hearing the Word, participating in the life of the church, receiving the promises. This shapes everything about how we parent.

### 4. Fathers Bear Final Responsibility

Not mothers. Not both parents equally. Fathers. Ephesians 6:4 says, "Fathers, do not provoke your children to wrath, but bring them up in the training and admonition of the Lord." The command is addressed to fathers. This does not minimize the mother's role — it is enormous. But it establishes where accountability ultimately rests.

### 5. Young Children Are Not Equipped for Independence

Our culture idolizes autonomy and self-expression, even in small children. The Bible does not. Young children do not have the wisdom, experience, or spiritual maturity to make their own decisions. They need guidance, boundaries, and authority — lovingly applied. Parents who abdicate this role are not being kind. They are failing their children.
